Why the real crisis is not artificial intelligence, but intellectual emptiness
People say artificial intelligence is making us think less.
There is some truth in that fear. If a student asks AI to write an essay and never reads it carefully, something has been lost. If an employee asks AI to produce a report and sends it without understanding the argument, thinking has been outsourced. If a writer asks AI to turn every vague intuition into a polished article, the result may look impressive while remaining hollow.
But this is not the whole story.
The deeper problem is more uncomfortable: AI is not only replacing thought. It is exposing how much of what we called thought was already empty.
It is revealing the difference between having an opinion and having an idea.
Between producing language and understanding what the language means.
Between sounding intelligent and actually thinking.
For years, weak thinking could hide behind effort. A long article looked serious because it took time to write. A corporate strategy document looked intelligent because it used the right terminology. A public statement sounded thoughtful…
